content.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems have more failure modes than a single language-model call. The retriever can return wrong documents. The context assembly can exceed the token budget or admit irrelevant chunks. The generator can ignore the context and confabulate. A single end-to-end pass/fail assertion catches none of these separately; you need a layered test suite that isolates each stage, gives fast feedback on every push, and runs the expensive LLM-judge gate only where it earns its cost.
Three Layers of a RAG Test Suite
Effective RAG testing splits into three orthogonal layers, each with its own fixture requirements, run frequency, and failure semantics.

attrs.alt: RAG test pipeline: Retrieval → Context Quality → E2E Answer → CI Gate

content.text: Noise chunks, token overrun, irrelevant context
Every RAG test layer runs against a
— a curated set of questions paired with known-relevant document IDs and expected answer fragments. Keep the golden set small (50–150 cases) so the retrieval and context layers remain fast.

# tests/rag/conftest.py
import pytest
from pathlib import Path
import json

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def rag_golden():
    """Load golden Q&A pairs with relevant document IDs."""
    path = Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures" / "golden.json"
    return json.loads(path.read_text())

# fixtures/golden.json shape:
# [
#   {
#     "question": "What is the refund policy?",
#     "relevant_doc_ids": ["doc-042", "doc-099"],
#     "expected_answer_contains": ["30 days", "original payment method"]
#   },
#   ...
# ]
The document corpus for the test index should be a minimal, self-contained snapshot — not a live production index. Store it in
as JSON files and rebuild the test index with a deterministic script whenever the fixture documents change.

# scripts/refresh_rag_index.py
"""Rebuild the test vector index from fixture documents."""
import json
from pathlib import Path
from app.indexer import Indexer

FIXTURE_DOCS = Path("tests/rag/fixtures/docs")
INDEX_PATH   = Path("tests/rag/fixtures/index")

def main():
    indexer = Indexer()
    docs = []
    for f in sorted(FIXTURE_DOCS.glob("*.json")):
        docs.extend(json.loads(f.read_text()))
    indexer.build(docs, output_path=INDEX_PATH)
    print(f"Indexed {len(docs)} documents -> {INDEX_PATH}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
The retrieval layer measures how well the vector search or BM25 step surfaces relevant documents before any generation happens. The two most informative metrics are
(fraction of relevant documents found in the top-K results) and
(how highly ranked the first relevant result is).

Thresholds to start with: Recall@5 ≥ 0.75 and MRR ≥ 0.55. Tune upward once you have production baselines. A drop in either metric while the generation quality stays constant is the fingerprint of an embedding model change, a chunk-size regression, or index corruption.
— new model shifts the vector space; old indexed embeddings become misaligned with query embeddings.
— doubling chunk size dilutes signal; halving it splits relevant passages across boundaries.
— adding a strict date or category filter silently removes relevant documents.
— new documents added to production but not to the test index, hiding newly needed recall cases.

Context quality tests run after retrieval returns documents but before they are sent to the generator. They check two properties: the assembled context must fit within the token budget, and the ratio of irrelevant chunks must stay below a threshold.

The noise ratio check is valuable even when retrieval recall is high. A retriever can return the right top-1 document and several irrelevant ones below it; if the context assembler takes all K results, the generator's attention is diluted. Keeping irrelevant chunks below 30 % is a practical starting point for most enterprise RAG systems.
RAGAS provides three metrics that map to context quality without requiring a golden expected answer:
(does the answer contain only claims supported by the context?),
(are the retrieved chunks relevant to the question?), and
(does the answer match a reference?). Install with

# tests/rag/test_ragas_metrics.py
from datasets import Dataset
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, context_relevancy, answer_correctness
from app.pipeline import rag_pipeline

def test_ragas_suite(rag_golden):
    rows = []
    for case in rag_golden:
        result = rag_pipeline(case["question"])
        rows.append({
            "question": case["question"],
            "answer": result.answer,
            "contexts": [c.text for c in result.context_chunks],
            "ground_truth": " ".join(case["expected_answer_contains"]),
        })

    ds = Dataset.from_list(rows)
    scores = evaluate(ds, metrics=[faithfulness, context_relevancy, answer_correctness])

    assert scores["faithfulness"] >= 0.80, f"Faithfulness {scores['faithfulness']:.3f}"
    assert scores["context_relevancy"] >= 0.70
    assert scores["answer_correctness"] >= 0.65

RAGAS calls an LLM internally for faithfulness scoring, so it belongs in the nightly suite rather than every commit. Cache the scores to a SQLite file and only re-evaluate cases whose input hash has changed.
The E2E layer calls the full RAG pipeline — retrieve, assemble, generate — and uses a judge model to score each answer on three binary criteria:
(no unsupported claims). Binary scoring per criterion is more reliable than a 1–5 scale because it removes the judge's calibration variance.
so the fast retrieval and context suites can run without them:
. Gate E2E tests on nightly schedules and on PRs that carry a
Wiring the Suites into GitHub Actions

Split RAG tests into two jobs. The retrieval and context job runs on every push to retrieval or pipeline code and is mandatory for merge. The E2E judge job runs on a nightly schedule and on PRs that need it explicitly.

# .github/workflows/rag-tests.yml
name: RAG CI

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'app/retrieval/**'
      - 'app/pipeline/**'
      - 'tests/rag/**'
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 3 * * *'  # nightly full suite

jobs:
  retrieval-and-context:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install -r requirements-test.txt
      - run: |
          pytest tests/rag/test_retrieval.py tests/rag/test_context_quality.py \
            -v --tb=short --junitxml=results/rag.xml
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: rag-results
          path: results/

  e2e-judge:  # nightly or PR label 'rag-eval'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'schedule' || contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'rag-eval')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install -r requirements-test.txt
      - env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          pytest tests/rag/test_e2e.py -m llmjudge \
            -v --tb=short --junitxml=results/e2e.xml
filter ensures the retrieval job does not fire on documentation or front-end changes. Add
to the trigger paths so that changes to fixtures or test code also re-run the suite.
Starting thresholds that work well for most production RAG systems — adjust based on your domain once you collect baseline data:
After production feedback shows missed relevant docs
After hallucination rate stays elevated despite generation changes
Before GA or after safety review

Store each test run's scores to a time-series table (Postgres or a simple JSON log). Plot them in a dashboard and set alert rules when a metric drops more than 5 % from the rolling 7-day mean. A sudden retrieval recall drop that precedes a faithfulness drop by a few days often traces to an index rebuild with a changed embedding model.
in git. If you add cases, tag the snapshot so historical scores stay comparable.
Never let the golden set go stale
. Rotate in real user questions from feedback every sprint; remove cases that match the current system trivially.
in CI. A judge model upgrade changes scores even when the system under test is unchanged — control for that separately.
Separate index fixture from production index
. Tests must be deterministic; a live index changes between runs and makes CI non-reproducible.
— skipping retrieval and context tests means a degraded retriever goes undetected until users complain.
— adding new questions without versioning makes it impossible to tell whether a score change is a regression or a dataset shift.
Using production API keys in CI
— gate E2E tests behind secrets and make them optional on contributor PRs to avoid accidental cost or key exposure.
— a RAG system that achieves high quality in 20 seconds is not production-ready. Add a p95 latency assertion to the E2E suite.
— if the generator cites a source, verify the source is in the returned context. A missing source test catches silent context truncation bugs.

Layer RAG tests into retrieval metrics (fast, every push), context quality (fast, every push), and E2E LLM-judge (expensive, nightly + PR label). Use a versioned golden dataset of 50–150 cases with known-relevant document IDs. Enforce Recall@5 ≥ 0.75, RAGAS faithfulness ≥ 0.80, and E2E grounded ≥ 0.90 as CI gate thresholds. Store per-run scores and alert on drift.
